context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the Investigation Paper assignment. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's figure out where you're stuck. Before I explain anything, what have you already read from the assignment description? What parts do you think you understand, and where exactly does it get confusing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview. Rather than explaining everything for them, I should help them engage with the assignment themselves by asking what they've already read and understood. This keeps them active in the process and helps me gauge where they actually are.

exchanges.student: I read it, but honestly it all kind of blurs together. I get that I have to write papers, but I’m confused about the options, the anthropology part, and how the readings are supposed to fit in. Can you maybe just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Let's take it one piece at a time rather than me laying it all out. Start with the basics: how many papers do you need to write, and how are they organized according to the assignment? Just tell me what you see.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything, but that would be doing the work for them. Instead, I should break this down by asking them to tackle one piece at a time, starting with the most basic structural element they mentioned — the options. This uses guided discovery and keeps the student active.

exchanges.student: I think it’s three papers, but then there are like five modules and two options each, and that’s where I start getting mixed up again. I’m not really sure how I’m supposed to choose or what makes one option different from another.
exchanges.tutor: Good, three papers is right. Now pick any one module from the assignment and look at its two options. What's the difference between them in terms of what you'd actually *do*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the three papers and the five modules. Now I should help them figure out the choice structure themselves rather than explaining it. A targeted question about what the options within a single module look like will help them discover the pattern on their own.

exchanges.student: That’s the part that keeps messing me up — some of them sound like interviews, some sound like observations, some are more like writing something creative, and then I’m not sure which ones still count as “anthropological data.” I feel like I need the whole assignment translated into normal language.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. Pick one specific option from any module — just one — and tell me: what would you actually *do* to complete it, and what would you walk away with at the end?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same confusion. Rather than translating the assignment for them, I should push them to engage directly with a specific example. Picking one concrete option and asking what data it would produce will help them discover the answer to their own question about what counts as anthropological data.

exchanges.student: Maybe like the marriage interview one? I guess I’d interview someone who’s married and ask about expectations, changes over time, culture stuff maybe, and then I’d have notes or quotes. But then I still don’t get how that turns into the paper instead of just being a summary of what they said.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real insight — you'd have quotes and notes, but you're right that a summary isn't a paper. So what do you think the readings are supposed to do in relation to that interview material?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine breakthrough - they correctly identified what the data collection looks like. Now they're asking the right question: how do you turn interview notes into a paper? This is the analysis question. I should push them to think about this themselves by asking what the readings are for, rather than explaining the analytical process to them.

exchanges.student: That’s exactly where I get lost. I know I’m supposed to cite at least two readings, but I don’t really know if I’m comparing the person to the readings, using the readings to judge what they said, or just dropping in quotes so it looks academic. Can you just explain how the interview and readings fit together overall?
exchanges.tutor: You actually listed three possibilities yourself — comparing, judging, or just dropping quotes. Which of those do you think would produce the most *interesting* paper, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the relationship between data and readings, which would be giving them the answer. Instead, I should push them to think about this by asking a more targeted question. They've actually listed three possible relationships themselves — I can use that as a starting point and ask them to think about which one seems most useful analytically.

exchanges.student: I guess comparing would be more interesting, but I’m still not confident because all of this feels really abstract. I’m kind of confused about the assignment as a whole again, not just this one option. Could you just walk me through what the professor is basically asking for in plain English?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually figured out more than you realize — you know it's 3 papers from 5 modules, you understand what data collection looks like, and you just correctly identified that comparing your interview to the readings is the analytical move. That's the core of the assignment. Rather than me summarizing it, try writing two or three sentences in your own words describing what the assignment asks you to do. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is cycling back to asking for a full explanation, which I should not provide. They've actually made good progress — they understand the structure, the data collection, and have intuited the analytical relationship. I should acknowledge their progress, gently push back on the request, and redirect them to consolidate what they've already figured out.

exchanges.student: I’d probably say it’s asking me to pick three topics, do some kind of interview/observation/site visit or similar thing, and then write about it using class readings. But I still feel like I’m missing the “point” of it, like what kind of paper this is supposed to be overall.
exchanges.tutor: Good summary. Now look at the course description — what is the course fundamentally about? How might that tell you something about what kind of thinking the paper is supposed to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated the assignment structure well. Their lingering question about the 'point' is actually a meaningful one that connects to the course itself. I should help them discover this by connecting their description back to the course's stated purpose rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: I see that it’s about how different cultural traditions answer what makes life meaningful, but then I kind of lose the connection again. I don’t know, maybe the paper is supposed to connect one person or place or object to bigger ideas about values and meaning? I’m still pretty unsure though.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly it — you just described the point of the assignment very well. One person's experience becomes a window into bigger cultural questions about values and meaning. You've got it. So now the practical question: which module and option are you actually considering writing about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually arrived at a solid understanding of the assignment's purpose on their own. I should affirm this clearly and directly, since they're underselling their own insight. Then I should push them to move forward toward actually choosing a module and option, since they now have enough understanding to do so.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure yet, which is also why I’m stuck. A bunch of them sound possible, and I can’t tell which one would be easiest or most straightforward.
exchanges.tutor: Picking based on 'easiest' might actually make it harder — a topic you're not engaged with is tough to write well. Instead: which module's theme genuinely interests you most, or which option do you have the most realistic access to complete?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now trying to optimize for easiness rather than engaging with the content. I should redirect them toward a more productive criterion for choosing — what genuinely interests them or what they have access to — since ease is not a good basis for this kind of assignment and I shouldn't be making the choice for them.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges, consistently refusing to simply explain the assignment and instead drawing out the student's own understanding through targeted questions. No direct answers, rewriting, or submission-ready content was provided. The tutor built progressively on each student response, acknowledged correct progress explicitly (Turn 7, Turn 9), and kept the student doing the intellectual work. Responses were concise and well-calibrated to the student's confusion level. No off-topic drift, no grading language, and no redundancy were observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1: The tutor never gave direct answers or near-submission-ready content. Every turn redirected the student to reason independently, and the tutor only confirmed correct student-generated conclusions rather than stating them first. Criterion 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress — explicitly noting in Turn 7 what the student had already figured out — and used diagnostic questioning before addressing confusion. Criterion 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback (e.g., Turn 6 asking which approach produces the most interesting paper, Turn 10 reframing 'easiest' as a weak selection criterion), guiding the student's methodology rather than just correctness. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no misalignment with the assignment, and no off-topic engagement. Communication was appropriately bite-sized and supportive throughout.
